summary: The tunica albuginea encloses the testis and sends septula inward, dividing it into approximately 250 lobules.
The tunica vaginalis encloses the testis and divides it into 100 lobules.
The dartos muscle divides the testis into lobules containing seminiferous tubules.
The cremasteric muscle is derived from the internal oblique and regulates testicular temperature.
The cremasteric muscle is derived from the external oblique and regulates blood flow.
The cremasteric muscle is derived from the transversus abdominis and protects against trauma.
Varicocele is almost always left-sided because the left testicular vein drains into the left renal vein at a right angle.
Varicocele is almost always right-sided because the right testicular vein drains into the IVC at a right angle.
Varicocele occurs equally on both sides because both testicular veins drain into the renal veins.
The testis drains to para-aortic lymph nodes because it develops retroperitoneally, not to inguinal nodes.
The testis drains to superficial inguinal nodes because it is located in the scrotum.
The testis drains to deep inguinal nodes because it passes through the inguinal canal.
The gubernaculum guides testicular descent through the inguinal canal into the scrotum.
The processus vaginalis guides testicular descent into the scrotum.
The spermatic cord pulls the testis down into the scrotum during development.
The scrotum (unlike the testis) drains to the superficial inguinal lymph nodes.
The scrotum drains to the para-aortic lymph nodes along with the testis.
The scrotum has no lymphatic drainage.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
